name: OWASP API Security Top 10 (2023)
description: The OWASP API Security Top 10 is a standard awareness document for API developers and security practitioners. It represents a broad consensus about the most critical security risks to APIs. The 2023 edition reflects the evolving API threat landscape, combining previously separate categories and introducing new risks such as Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows and Unsafe Consumption of APIs. Organizations building or consuming APIs should use this document to ensure their API security programs address these critical risks.

controls.title: Broken Object Level Authorization
controls.description: Attackers exploit API endpoints vulnerable to broken object-level authorization by manipulating the ID of an object sent within the request. Object IDs can be sequential integers, UUIDs, or generic strings, making them straightforward targets for manipulation in request parameters, headers, or payloads. This issue is extremely common in API applications because the server component usually does not fully track the client's state and relies on parameters like object IDs. Unauthorized access can lead to data disclosure, data loss, data manipulation, and in some cases full account takeover.
controls.discussion: Implement a proper authorization mechanism that relies on user policies and hierarchy. Use the authorization mechanism to check if the logged-in user has access to perform the requested action on the record in every function that uses client input. Prefer the use of random and unpredictable values as GUIDs for records' IDs, and write tests to evaluate the vulnerability of the authorization mechanism.

controls.title: Broken Authentication
controls.description: Authentication mechanisms are easy targets for attackers since they are exposed to everyone. Issues stem from misconceptions regarding authentication boundaries and inherent implementation complexity, making authentication problems widespread and straightforward to detect. Attackers can gain complete control of other users' accounts, read their personal data, and perform sensitive actions on their behalf.
controls.discussion: Implement rate limiting stricter than regular API limits on authentication endpoints and deploy anti-brute force mechanisms addressing credential stuffing and dictionary attacks. Require re-authentication for sensitive operations, use multi-factor authentication where feasible, and treat password recovery flows with the same protections as login endpoints. Avoid reinventing authentication — use standards, reject unsigned or weakly signed JWT tokens, and validate token expiration dates.

controls.title: Broken Object Property Level Authorization
controls.description: This vulnerability occurs when APIs expose properties of an object that are considered sensitive and should not be read by the user, or allow a user to change, add, or delete the value of a sensitive object's property which the user should not be able to access. This can result in unauthorized data access, data manipulation, or in severe cases privilege escalation or account compromise. It combines the previously separate concerns of Excessive Data Exposure and Mass Assignment from the 2019 edition.
controls.discussion: When exposing an object using an API endpoint, always verify that the user should have access to the specific properties being exposed. Avoid using generic methods such as to_json() and to_string() — instead cherry-pick specific object properties. Avoid automatic binding of client input to internal objects (mass assignment), allow changes only to properties that should be updated by the client, and implement a schema-based response validation mechanism as an extra layer of security.

controls.title: Unrestricted Resource Consumption
controls.description: An API is vulnerable if it is missing or has inappropriately configured limits on execution timeouts, maximum allocable memory, number of file descriptors, number of processes, upload file size, number of operations per request, records per page to return, or third-party service spending limits. Exploitation leads to denial of service through resource starvation and unexpected operational cost increases from higher infrastructure demands.
controls.discussion: Use containerization or serverless solutions that make it easy to limit memory, CPU, restarts, file descriptors, and processes. Define and enforce a maximum size of data on all incoming parameters and payloads, and implement rate limiting on how often a client can interact with the API within a defined timeframe. Add proper server-side validation for query string and request body parameters, and configure spending limits for all service provider and API integrations.

controls.title: Broken Function Level Authorization
controls.description: Exploitation requires the attacker to send legitimate API calls to an API endpoint that they should not have access to as anonymous users or regular, non-privileged users. Authorization checks for a function or resource are usually managed via configuration or code level and implementing proper checks can be complex with modern role hierarchies. Such flaws allow attackers to access unauthorized functionality — administrative functions are key targets and may lead to data disclosure, data loss, or data corruption.
controls.discussion: The enforcement mechanism should deny all access by default, requiring explicit grants to specific roles for access to every function. Review API endpoints for authorization flaws while considering business logic and group hierarchy. Ensure administrative controllers inherit from abstract controllers implementing authorization checks based on user roles, and verify that administrative functions in regular controllers implement authorization checks based on user groups and roles.

controls.title: Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows
controls.description: Exploitation involves understanding the business model backed by the API, finding sensitive business flows, and automating access to these flows in a way that causes harm to the business. The vulnerability occurs when endpoints expose sensitive business operations without appropriate access restrictions. Examples include purchasing flows vulnerable to scalping, comment or posting systems susceptible to spam, and reservation systems where attackers can monopolize availability.
controls.discussion: Identify business workflows that could suffer harm from excessive automated use and implement mechanisms to slow automated threats. Use device fingerprinting to deny service to unexpected client devices (e.g., headless browsers), deploy human detection via captcha or advanced biometric solutions, and analyze user flow to detect non-human patterns. Consider blocking IP addresses of Tor exit nodes and well-known proxies, and restrict access to machine-consumed APIs.

controls.title: Server Side Request Forgery
controls.description: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) flaws occur when an API fetches a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL. This enables attackers to manipulate applications into sending crafted requests to unintended destinations, potentially bypassing firewalls or VPNs. The risk is amplified by modern development practices like webhooks, URL previews, and file fetching from URLs, combined with cloud infrastructure that exposes management and metadata interfaces over HTTP on predictable, well-known paths.
controls.discussion: Isolate the resource fetching mechanism in your network, since these features are typically aimed at retrieving remote resources, not internal ones. Implement allowlists for remote origins, URL schemes, ports, and accepted media types. Disable HTTP redirections and use a well-tested and maintained URL parser to avoid issues caused by URL parsing inconsistencies. Validate and sanitize all client-supplied input data, and do not send raw responses to clients.

controls.title: Security Misconfiguration
controls.description: Security misconfiguration can happen at any level of the API stack, from the network level to the application level. The vulnerability occurs when appropriate security hardening is absent, security patches are missing, unnecessary features remain enabled, or systems lack proper configuration across infrastructure components. Specific vulnerabilities include missing TLS encryption, improperly configured CORS policies, disabled security headers, exposed error messages revealing sensitive details, and discrepancies in how HTTP servers in the chain process requests.
controls.discussion: Establish a repeatable hardening process and ensure all API communications happen over an encrypted communication channel (TLS). Restrict HTTP verbs to only those necessary for business functions, implement proper CORS policies and security headers for browser-based clients, and define and enforce all API response payload schemas to prevent exception traces from being sent to attackers. Review configurations across the entire API stack including orchestration files and cloud services.

controls.title: Improper Inventory Management
controls.description: This vulnerability stems from organizational failure to maintain awareness of their API landscape. Outdated documentation makes it more difficult to find or fix vulnerabilities, and lack of asset inventory and retirement strategies leads to running unpatched systems, resulting in leakage of sensitive data. Organizations also lack visibility into data sharing with third parties — APIs may share sensitive data without proper business justification, inventory tracking, or transparency about what data transfers occur.
controls.discussion: Inventory all API hosts and document important aspects of each, focusing on the API environment (production, staging, test, development). Inventory integrated services and document their role in the system and what data is exchanged. Generate documentation automatically by adopting open standards, and use external protection measures such as API-security-specific solutions for all exposed versions. Avoid using production data in non-production deployments, and conduct risk analysis when newer API versions include security improvements.

controls.title: Unsafe Consumption of APIs
controls.description: Developers tend to trust data received from third-party APIs more than user input, adopting weaker security standards for validation and sanitization. The vulnerability exists when APIs interact with external services over unencrypted channels, fail to properly validate and sanitize third-party data, blindly follow redirects, lack resource consumption limits for third-party interactions, or have no timeout mechanisms. Successful exploitation may result in sensitive data exposure, various injection attacks, or denial of service.
controls.discussion: When evaluating service providers, assess their API security posture. Ensure all API interactions happen over a secure communication channel (TLS) and always validate and properly sanitize data received from integrated APIs before using it. Maintain an allowlist of well-known locations that integrated APIs may redirect yours to — do not blindly follow redirects.
